text: The surgeon carefully dissects soft tissue from the interlateral trigon to preserve the hypogastric nerve. Dissection proceeds along the ventral side of the abdominal aorta, with gentle traction around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The hypogastric nerve is preserved at the IMA root, and the IMA is transected 1-2 centimeters away from the root. Soft tissues are separated and pushed down to expose the area, followed by double clipping with hemoclips on the proximal side and a 10mm endoclip on the distal side of the IMA.

The surgeon uses an energy device to transect a vessel, followed by dissection along the areolar tissue between the sigmoid mesocolon and the gel autophagia. The medial approach provides ample working space, minimizing handling of the sigmoid colon. The dissection reveals the ureter, dumbbells, and long dorsal vessels, continuing at the transition from the mesosigmoid to the mesorectum to achieve safe margins for transection.

The surgeon releases tension along the left paracolic gutter and dissects along the left side of the pelvis. After dissection, the anatomical relationship is visible, highlighting the importance of preserving the hypogastric nerve at the sacral promontory level, which typically lies one to two centimeters medial to the ureter.

Using the robotic excise system, the surgeon clears the soft tissue surrounding the sigmoid colon. After ensuring no surrounding structures are trapped, the GIA stapler is used for transection, leaving a visible proximal and distal stump of the sigmoid colon.
